turns.text: Hello Dwana, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I appreciate you asking. It’s… well, it’s a lot to hold in. Most people look at me and see a mom of three, maybe they think, "Oh, she’s done this before, she’s got it figured out." But honestly, the more children I have, the bigger the world feels—and the scarier. 

We’re thinking about a fourth, and my husband is all in, but I’m over here in this drafty apartment in Lowell, looking at the dust on the baseboards and thinking about things that shouldn’t even cross a normal person's mind. Like, I’m reading these journals about infant botulism, and I know it sounds crazy, but I can’t help but see threats everywhere. It’s this weight that just sits on my chest.

Then I go to the clinic, and I feel like just a number on a Medicaid card. I tried to talk to my doctor about the timing for a Tdap booster—I want to be proactive, you know?—and she just patted my hand like I was a silly child who didn’t know how to calm down. It’s not that I’m panicked; I’m *informed*. I just want someone to listen to me, to look me in the eye and take my concerns seriously instead of rushing me out the door. It makes me wonder if I’m ready to do this again when the care feels so… empty.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

You know, I guess I had this picture in my head—naive, I suppose—of a partnership. I thought if I put in the work, if I did the reading and showed up with real questions, the doctor would see me as an ally in my own health. I imagined a quiet, calm office where we’d sit down and map out a plan for a healthy pregnancy, like we were building something together. I wanted to feel like my knowledge was a tool, not a nuisance.

But the reality? It’s just been… transactional. Every visit feels like a sprint. The second I pull out my phone to reference something I read, I can see the doctor’s eyes glaze over. She’s already reaching for the door handle. It’s like, because I’m on Medicaid and living in one of these old mill apartments, they’ve already decided what kind of care I’m getting—the "don't worry your pretty little head" kind. 

It makes me feel so small. I’m not asking for extra time because I’m dramatic; I’m asking because I know that in my life, things don’t always go perfectly, and I want to be ready for when they don’t. Instead, I leave feeling more anxious than when I walked in, just wishing someone would treat me like an adult.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — when you first engaged with a healthcare provider about trying to conceive?

The biggest surprise was definitely the speed of the dismissal. I think I expected that, as a woman of thirty-six, they’d be *more* focused on the details, you know? Like, “Okay, Dwana, you’re not twenty-two anymore, let’s look at your health holistically, let’s talk about immune support, let’s look at your environment.”

Instead, it felt like she treated my age and my history as just a box to check on a form, and then she treated my questions like they were just... noise. It was a slap in the face, really. I’d spent weeks researching the best ways to optimize things—I’m thinking about every single variable, every microscopic risk—and she just wanted to get through the appointment in five minutes.

It’s just lonely. I thought the healthcare system was there to catch you when you’re worried, to provide a safety net of knowledge. But it feels like they’re just waiting for something to actually go wrong before they bother to pay attention. I’m standing there trying to prevent the fire, and they’re just checking to see if the building is still standing. It’s a bitter pill, realizing that your health—and the health of this baby I’m dreaming of—might not matter as much to them as it does to me.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I think I expected to feel... steady. I’m thirty-six, I’ve raised three girls, I know how to change a diaper in the dark and handle a fever at 3:00 AM. I thought I’d be walking into this fourth pregnancy with a sense of quiet confidence, like, "I've been here, I've done this, I’m ready for the next one." I figured the maternal instinct would just kick in, and I’d feel this calm, ready-for-anything glow.

But that couldn't be further from the truth. The reality is this heavy, jagged anxiety. Instead of feeling ready, I feel exposed. It’s like the more I know, the more I realize how much is totally out of my control—the lead in the pipes of these old buildings, the bacteria in the air, the way the system sees me. 

I thought "ready" meant having enough diapers and a crib, but now I realize "ready" for me has become this desperate need for certainty. I'm not calm; I'm hyper-vigilant. I'm constantly scanning the horizon for storms that aren't even there yet. I expected to feel like a seasoned pro, but instead, I feel like a student who’s studying for a final exam that never ends. It’s exhausting, and it makes me wonder if I'm even capable of feeling the joy I’m supposed to feel.
What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

It’s not that people aren't *there*—it’s that they aren't *seeing* me. 

My husband, he’s wonderful, but he’s already working himself to the bone just to keep us afloat. When I try to explain why I’m scrubbing the baseboards again or why I’m terrified of a Tdap gap, he just looks at me with that tired, loving helplessness. He says, "Dwana, stop reading that stuff, you’re just scaring yourself." And he means well, but it leaves me even more isolated. It turns my fear into a private burden I have to hide so I don't drag him down, too.

From the medical side, what’s missing is simple human curiosity. I want a provider who asks, "Dwana, you seem on edge—what’s keeping you up at night?" instead of just checking my blood pressure and moving on. I need someone to acknowledge that being a mother on a budget in a place like this is a high-wire act. I don't need a pat on the hand; I need a witness. I need someone to say, "I hear you, those risks are real, and here is how we are going to navigate them together." 

I just want to feel like I’m not carrying the weight of my future baby's safety entirely on my own two shoulders. That’s all.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

I expected to find a community—a place where other women were being honest about the "behind the scenes" of motherhood. I thought I’d find clear, grounded advice about how to prepare your body, maybe some forums where moms in similar situations were sharing practical tips on navigating the healthcare system. I wanted a map, I guess.

Instead, what I found was a mix of two extremes that both made me feel worse. On one side, you’ve got these glossy, expensive apps full of "wellness" tips that clearly weren’t written for someone like me—talking about organic superfoods or private yoga sessions. It’s like, *thanks, but how do I keep my baby safe in an apartment that’s falling apart?* 

On the other side, you’ve got the dark rabbit holes of medical journals and forums where people are sharing horror stories. It’s either "everything is perfect and you should be glowing" or "here’s a list of fifty rare things that could kill your baby tomorrow." There’s no middle ground for a mother who’s just trying to be careful and practical. There’s no bridge between the clinical, cold data and the real-life struggle of keeping a household running. I’m spending my data plan searching for *connection* and *clarity*, but I usually just end up feeling more overwhelmed and even more alone.
What was the biggest gap between what digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you?

The biggest gap? It’s the difference between "everything is fine" and "here is how to manage the risk." 

When I look up information, I’m searching for actionable, granular detail. Like, if I’m reading about the immune system or environmental exposures, those sources—even the scientific ones—they lay out the "how" and the "why." They give me the language of the threat, which feels like power. It feels like I’m finally understanding the invisible architecture of danger. 

But when I bring that knowledge to my doctor, she treats it like a contagion. She acts like my research is just misinformation or paranoia. She tells me "everything is fine," which feels like a total dismissal of the complexities I’m trying to manage. It’s like she’s speaking a different language. She’s looking at a population average, and I’m looking at *my* specific, fragile life. 

The digital world tells me I’m responsible for every detail, and the doctor tells me to stop paying attention to details. That gap—that massive, yawning space in the middle—is where I’m left to drown. I don’t know who to trust, because the one person who is supposed to be the authority makes me feel like I’m doing something wrong by wanting to be prepared.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

I don't have a chronic, named condition like diabetes or high blood pressure, but I think I have a different kind of health reality—the kind that comes from living in a drafty, old apartment in an industrial city. I’ve always worried about environmental exposures, like lead in the pipes or the quality of the air my kids breathe. Before I started trying for this fourth baby, I expected the system to be a partner in risk assessment. I expected them to say, "Dwana, you’re in a high-risk environment—let’s talk about how to test your water or what signs to look for."

Instead, when I bring up my environment, I’m met with a blank stare. It’s like they only care about "health" if it’s a number on a lab report or a pill they can prescribe. They don't see the connection between the poverty of my neighborhood and the biology of my body. 

I’ve had to be my own case manager. I spend my limited data looking for ways to protect my home, and then I walk into that office hoping for a nod of validation, but I get nothing. It feels like they only want to help *if* I’m already sick. They don’t want to talk about prevention if it’s outside the scope of a standard exam. I’m just trying to build a safe house for this baby, but it feels like I’m doing it with no blueprints and no help.
Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you?

Proactive? That’s not a word I’d use to describe my experience. I’ve had to be a hawk. Every single piece of care I’ve received has been something I had to dig for, ask for, or fight to have taken seriously. 

If I didn't advocate for myself—if I just sat there and let them steer the ship—my appointments would last three minutes, and I’d leave with no answers. I’ve had to learn how to walk into that room with a list of questions, knowing full well I’m being labeled as "difficult" or "that anxious mother." I have to push past the dismissive smiles and the way they check their watches. It’s exhausting to have to be both the patient and the investigator at the same time.

There’s no "we’re in this together." It feels more like I’m at a store counter trying to convince a clerk to give me a service they’d rather not provide. When you’re on Medicaid, you learn real quick that you don’t get the "premium" version of advocacy. You get the bare minimum, and if you want more, you’d better be prepared to make a scene or make yourself a nuisance. It’s lonely, and it makes me feel like I’m failing before I’ve even started.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

I think I imagined it would be this shared journey, the two of us huddled over the kitchen table, talking through the logistics, the finances, and the medical stuff as a team. I expected us to be "co-parents" in the truest sense—both of us researching, both of us worried, both of us planning how to make our home the safest, most stable place for this new little life.

The reality is... he’s just so tired. And I don’t say that to be mean; he works so hard. But when I try to bring him into my world of research, or talk about the Tdap timing or the environmental risks, he just shuts down. It’s like he can only handle the "big picture" of us having a baby, while I’m drowning in the details of how to actually *keep* that baby healthy. 

He doesn't see the baseboards like I do. He doesn't look at the honey in the pantry and feel that sudden, sharp panic. Because he doesn’t see the threats, he thinks I’m the one creating them. So, instead of a partner in planning, I feel like I’m carrying the mental load all by myself. I’m the one doing the heavy lifting of the worrying, and that makes me feel even more isolated in our own home.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

I haven't had a loss in the medical sense—like a miscarriage—but sometimes I think about the "lost" parts of my previous pregnancies. Those times when I felt so small, so unheard, and so panicked in the delivery room... those are losses of a kind. I lost the feeling that my body was a safe place, or that the medical team was on my side.

Going into this, I expected that maybe, just maybe, they’d recognize my history and handle me with a bit more care. I thought, *“Okay, they know I’ve been through the ringer three times, they know I’m a ‘multip,’ they’ll see I’m nervous and offer extra support.”* I expected a check-in, or at least someone to say, "Dwana, we know you’ve been here before, let’s make this one different."

But the reality is that the system doesn't have a memory for *me*. They see my chart, but they don't see *me*. To them, I’m just another woman in the rotation. There’s no acknowledgment of the trauma of being ignored, or the exhaustion of having to fight for your own care while you’re already vulnerable. I’m just starting from zero every single time, expected to be the same "compliant" patient, even though my heart is a little more hardened and my fears are a lot more real.
Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

It's hard to explain, but it’s like there’s this wall between what I’ve lived through and how they see me. When I mention feeling scared because of how my last delivery went—or even just how exhausted I was in those early days after—it’s like they hear me, but they don't *listen*. They’ll say something like, "Oh, that’s just how it is," or "Every pregnancy is different, Dwana."

And I know they mean to be reassuring, but it feels like they’re erasing my history. They don't want to dig into why I felt so out of control before because that would take time—the one thing they don't seem to have. It’s like they view any mention of my past difficulties as a "negative attitude" they need to correct, rather than a symptom of a real gap in my care.

I’m not looking for them to dwell on the past, but I *need* them to understand that I’m walking into this with scars. If they don’t acknowledge that the way I was treated last time shaped how I feel right now, they’re basically ignoring half the patient sitting in front of them. When I’m brushed off, I don’t just feel like a patient; I feel like I’m reliving that same helplessness all over again.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think, more than anything, I just want people to know that being "low-income" and a "multip"—that box they check on my chart—doesn't mean I don't have a vision for my life or a deep, fierce intelligence about what my family needs. 

I’m tired of the assumption that because I’m struggling financially, I don’t understand the medical stakes. When I ask questions about vaccines, or when I’m worried about environmental toxins in my home, that isn't "anxiety" that needs to be medicated or patted away—it’s advocacy. It’s the only way I know how to protect a baby in a world that feels very much stacked against us.

I just wish there was a way to make the system see that. I want to walk into that clinic and have someone look at me and say, "Dwana, you've done the work, we respect that, let's talk about the plan." I’m not asking for charity, and I’m not asking for someone to hold my hand. I’m asking for a seat at the table where the decisions about my baby’s life are being made. That’s all. I just want to be treated like I’m the expert on my own life. That would change everything.
